title: How to safely use a BD syringe for dermal filler.
filename: How to safely use a BD syringe for dermal filler..en.vtt
excerpt: So why would I use a BD syringe instead of a normal dermal filler syringe we had lots of questions about this so I thought I'd do a full video comparing and contrasting the different devices so that you can make a better decision about when to use each don't forget to stay to the…

segments.text: so why would I use a BD syringe instead of a normal dermal filler syringe we had lots of questions about this so I thought I'd do a full video comparing and contrasting the different devices so that you can make a better decision about when to use each don't forget to stay to the end because I have a very special guest joining me from Australia who's going to help us understand some of the important differences between regulation in the UK and Australia and then and in America and why that might be different if you want to use a BD syringe so in this clip you can see me using a BD syringe in the nose the reason I often use a BD syringe in noses is because I'm often not using a full meal it's quite useful if you're doing an adjustment or a small treatment to just use a small amount of that product this also gives you greater control when injecting and this for me is the biggest difference between BD syringes and the devices that the dermal fillers come with which is the amount of movement for the plunger in a BD syringe is much more for a smaller volume so in the case of noses I like to use small amounts a

little and often so to speak so small injection Checker pulley refill and this is the easiest way to avoid the main thing that people worry about with noses which would be blindness you need to inject at least 0.085 mils to have any chance of filling the vessel all the way back to the eye which is what everyone worries most about so the easiest way to reduce that is to only inject 0.05 and the best way to control that is to use a device that is very clear about how much you're injecting the difference between a normal dermal filler syringe and a BD syringe is probably about twice as much movement required on the BD syringe to put the same amount of product in so if you have a look at the smallest gradient on this syringe that is equal to this whole space in the BD syringe so compressing this from here to there would be the same as this whole movement here if you actually look at the distance the plunger needs to move with a BD syringe compared with a normal

filler syringe it's about two and a half times further for the same amount of product effectively you could say that it's two and a half times more accurate to use a BD syringe does using a BD actually alter the result I don't think it Alters the result but it gives you more certainty when you're injecting about exactly how much you're injecting because one of the problems with these devices that would that the product comes with is that you're often looking from different angles so if you imagine looking down with the needle from this position it's actually even harder to see how much movement there is in a plunger so what you get as an injector is a greater degree of certainty with how much you're injecting there's less plastic involved with the BD as well so your plunger is closer to the wall of the syringe so you can actually see more clearly where the plunger is compared with one of these particularly at an angle and the amount of movement to get the same result is so much more as we've discussed so this overall in my mind creates a greater sense of certainty about what you're actually doing during procedures and it's particularly useful

in any procedure where you're very concerned about putting a small amount in at a time so typically this would be tear trough some of the details in ellipse and of course noses so if I like Beady syringes so much why am I not using them in every area well it's mainly for the reasons I've just said a lot of the areas that I treat I don't really need that much discernment between different volumes it's okay to be within a 10 error margin when you're injecting larger amounts in areas that are basically less sensitive to Tiny differences so what about the risk of vascular occlusion do you increase the risk of a vo by using a BD syringe well the reason that I use BD syringes is actually typically to reduce the severity of vascular occlusion it doesn't necessarily reduce the frequency but I know they'll be smaller so one of the other downsides of using a BD syringe that some people discover is that the compression force the Extrusion Force require to inject can be much higher particularly on thicker dermal fillers so this is one of the natural changes that happens to do with changing

the physics of the syringe the amount of movement for the same reason as it increases your accuracy when you've got a thicker product means you've got to push harder for longer to get the same result so this could be one reason why you might Reserve using BD syringes for lower viscosity products or you may just decide to go to the gym and exercise your thumb a bit more because it really is just a matter of pressure so one of the things I've noticed on social media is that the Australians seem to respond very differently to some of my videos than the rest of the world and I'm getting the idea over time that you guys are much more regulated and controlled than other parts of the world so I thought I'd get an expert on the subject matter someone who's got a fantastic point of view from two different countries which is Jake Sloan Jake obviously originated in the UK but also now works and lives in Australia and so he can give us an excellent insight into the differences but between regulation at least between those two countries so I thought I'd ask him some questions maybe I'll give a bit of a backstory because I think it will help color the whole conversation so

whole conversation so um unlike the UK uh you know I've obviously worked in the UK and injected in the UK but having moved here it's obviously abundantly clear that there are different regulatory expectations and guidelines to work within and you know traditionally we sort of look at the UK as being pretty loose and you know there's not too much regulation whereas you know here it's quite different so I guess it's probably hot on people's minds here in Australia as well because we've just gone through another round of extra regulation or a sort of a reading of the rules um so everyone is sort of on high alert to do the right thing I guess um that was you know there's lots of nuances to those new regulations but they kicked on July 1st of this year so not that long ago um so in reference to your question about BD syringes I mean just from a forget which country in I think it just sort of boils down to a simple fact that if you have a pre-filled syringe with a supplied needle or cannula from A supplier let's say I don't know what's the not username but a big Pharma

company and then you change the parameters of that and use your own needle own syringe and potentially during that decanting process there's an issue of potential sterility or no sterility then I think it's just logical to argue that maybe that's not how it was designed you know forget that forget the law in the country so I guess here in Australia there will be injectors um sort of looking at practice and maybe they're trying to do you know the right thing because you know the spotlight is on injectors to do the right thing with the new regulations um the way it works here in the uh sorry in Australia is that each injector either is a doctor a cosmetic doctor we have obviously registered nurses we have nurse practitioners who are a little bit more autonomous so they can prescribe for themselves like the doctors but registered nurses work under the supervision of a prescriber so you know if a registered nurse decided to go off label or off-piste if

you like that would sort of have to have been discussed with the prescriber because the prescriber's job is to look at the patient talk to the patient they come up with a treatment plan and normally it's done virtually on a zoom call or a FaceTime call and then you know the nurse institutes the treatment so if the prescriber doesn't deem that you know uh kosher if you like then by definition you can't for patients um who want to have surgery with a plastic surgeon or indeed a cosmetic surgeon and they have to be referred through their GP whereas you know traditionally if I saw a patient on my bed and I thought oh you're you know you're appropriate for a blepharoplasty I will you know verbally or formally send you to a colleague who does blepharoplasty I can't do that now I'm not allowed to get involved they have to go through their general practitioner which has caused you know a lot of um anger because of course it clogs up the GPS with all these people just wanting cosmetic surgery referrals and of course the general practitioners

don't feel um like they have an understanding of what they're fending for you know that's not their territory often so it's sort of created a bit of a roadblock for patients and surgeons to be connected you know there's a few other things and one of the other ones was that patients wanting to have cosmetic surgery um sort of by definition have to go through a psychological screening now which you know won't go into the sort of the details but it feels like a bit of an overreach but a good surgeon would argue they would have done that anyway if they're due diligence to check someone's mental capacity before an operation so anyway whereas that was when these guidelines were being sort of um announced but hadn't quite come into play there was a fear that would also be the case for us as injectors and you know it sort of puts a lot of your patience on the back foot to basically saying right I'm going to go through these mental health questionnaire now to deem you uh worthy of having Botox

of having Botox um in practice of course I don't yeah but you know in practice you know I was sort of doing my own screening process anyway prior to the law change so from my perspective I don't feel much has changed but what I am doing is using a validated questionnaire now so again if anything ever happened medically legally I can actually say well I used a validated screening tool that's the best we've got and of course I spoke to the patient face to face I guess the nurses will probably feel the more the new regulations more than you know myself you know I'm a prescriber I'm a doctor and so even though you know there's a few sort of additional things that I have to do with my pages now that I wasn't doing before really in practice it's same old my patients could have some forms we have a consult and if they're appropriate and they agree we do a treatment whereas with the nurse it you know sorry the prescriber sort of looking after the nurse it does feel like there are a few more hurdles a little bit more formality to the process and of course if things go wrong

you know there's more responsibility of the doctor or the prescriber to sort of get it involved so yeah I think it has maybe changed but I you know there's always a fear when new regulations come in that you're doing the wrong thing so there was always the anxiety that you know you might get audited by the regulator or whatever but I think things have settled down and people have realized okay it's not a huge change and actually maybe some of the regulatory changes that have made things a little bit more um black and white as to what is expected it's actually good for the patient um you know rather than sort of casually uh you know going through the motions when you're Consulting you know there are certain things that just have to be done so you could argue that it's good but um yeah I think it the changes were more for those registered nurses under the supervision of a prescribing yeah the next thing to consider might be whether there is something in BD syringes that could negatively affect filler now I don't think there's any clear evidence that this is the case but there is a theory that silicon oils used

on the inside of syringes which may even be on the inside of filler syringes can trigger inflammatory responses if you haven't seen this video there's an interesting study published by Dr Maria Landau in this video I discussed this idea of morphea-like lesions which are caused potentially from Silicon oils inside BD syringes used to inject botulin toxins and what's what this results in is a little small indentation the patients get after being injected with botulent toxin it's very rare I've seen one in my career but it is a potential risk with dermal and the same oils now I've yet to find a patient that I can definitely identify as reactor to dermal filler after a BD syringe injection but that is a theoretical risk that you may want to consider I don't think it's high risk at all and that's why I continue to use BD syringes but it is worth thinking about you can watch that video on morphea-like lesions here and I hope you found the rest of this video interesting and helped you make a better decision about whether you'd like to use BD syringes or not
